slides.label: Cold Open
slides.text: Shift change. The outgoing crew walks toward the exit. One of them pulls a lock off a disconnect on their way out. Casual. Done for the day.

slides.label: Scene
slides.text: The disconnect has no lock now. The incoming crew is still in the break room getting briefed. For the next four minutes, this machine is unprotected.

slides.heading:
Four minutes.
That's the gap between
one crew's safety and the next.
slides.body: And in that gap, anyone could start this machine. Shift change creates vulnerability unless managed properly.

slides.heading:
Seven steps to
bring it back safely
slides.body: You've locked the machine out. You've done the work. Now it's time to restore the equipment. This has its own sequence, and it matters as much as the lockout itself.

items.text: Inspect: Work is finished, all tools accounted for
Clean up: Parts, rags, work aids back where they belong
Replace guards: Every guard back before machine starts
Check controls: Everything in neutral or safest position
Check for personnel: All workers verified clear and notified
Remove your lock: Only your lock. Not someone else's.

slides.label: Check for Personnel
slides.boxText: This is not a glance. This is a headcount.
slides.body: If three people were working on this machine, three people must be verified clear before any lock comes off. Every person who was working must be accounted for and notified.

slides.heading:
Multiple workers,
multiple locks
slides.body: When multiple workers need to service the same equipment, each one applies their own lock. The principle scales: one person, one lock. The machine cannot restart until the last lock is off.

slides.label: Group Coordinator
slides.boxText: Someone needs to be in charge. One authorized employee manages the overall lockout.
slides.body: They don't replace individual locks. They organize the process, review procedures with everyone, and coordinate the work.

slides.heading:
What if there's only
room for one lock?
slides.body: One lock goes on the disconnect. The key to that lock goes inside a lockbox. Each worker puts their own lock on the lockbox. The machine can't start until every worker removes their lock from the box.

slides.heading:
Continuous protection
across shift changes
slides.body: The incoming crew applies their locks before the outgoing crew removes theirs. Overlap protection. The machine is never unprotected, not even for minutes.

slides.heading:
When someone leaves
their lock behind
slides.body: This requires special authorization. The absent employee must be contacted and verified. Management approval required. All affected personnel notified. This is not a casual decision.

slides.label: Roles and Responsibility
slides.boxText: Authorized employee: Can apply and remove locks, coordinate group lockout. Affected employee: Notified of lockout, must stay clear.
slides.body: Each role has specific responsibilities. Know which role you're in for each lockout situation. Authority matters. Clear communication matters.

slides.heading:
Documentation isn't
bureaucracy
slides.body: It's memory. Who's working where. What energy sources are controlled. When the work started. When it ended. When someone gets hurt, this documentation explains what went right and what went wrong.

slides.heading:
LOTO isn't just
a six-step procedure
slides.body: It's a comprehensive system. Individual lockout. Group lockout. Shift change protocols. Emergency procedures. Documentation. Each element protects against specific failure modes.

slides.heading:
You now understand
the energy you don't see
slides.body: Five types of hazardous energy. Six steps to control them. Protocols for complex situations. The tools to protect yourself and your coworkers. Two minutes and a padlock. That's all it takes.

slides.heading: LOTO Course — Key Concepts
items: LOTO is a comprehensive system, not just a procedure
Group lockout scales the one-person-one-lock principle
Emergency lock removal needs special authorization
Everyone goes home safe — every shift, every day

quiz.question: In group lockout situations with multiple workers, how many locks should be applied?
options: One lock controlled by the supervisor
Two locks — one for electrical, one for mechanical
One lock per person working on the equipment
The minimum number required to secure all energy sources

quiz.question: What is the lockbox method used for?
options: Storing extra padlocks for future use
When multiple workers need lockout but only one lock fits the disconnect
Emergency storage of critical equipment keys
Group lockout situations with more than 5 workers

quiz.question: During shift change, when should the incoming crew apply their locks?
options: After the outgoing crew removes all their locks
Before the outgoing crew removes their locks (overlap protection)
Only if work will continue into the next shift

quiz.question: What step comes immediately before removing locks during equipment restoration?
options: Check for personnel — verify all workers are clear
